---
title: "Prep Work: Linear Functions and Equations"
format: 
  live-html:
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .btn-exercise-solution { display: none !important; }
        </style>
    webr:
      packages:
        - dplyr
        - gradethis
        - jsonlite
        - base64enc
      cell-options: 
        persist: true
    live:
      show-solutions: true
---
{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}
{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_gradethis.qmd >}}

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
problemRegistry = ({
  "q1_plot_points":       "q1_plot_points",
  "q2_slope_two_points":  "q2_slope_two_points",
  "q3_slope_two_points_again": "q3_slope_two_points_again",
  "q4_evaluate_function": "q4_evaluate_function",
  "q5_evaluate_and_solve": "q5_evaluate_and_solve"
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
mutable momScores = Object.fromEntries(
    Object.values(problemRegistry).map(d => [d, "unattempted"])
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
{
  window.addEventListener("message", function(event) {
    if (event.origin !== "https://www.myopenmath.com") return;

    let data;
    try {
      data = typeof event.data === "string" ? JSON.parse(event.data) : event.data;
    } catch(e) { return; }

    if (data.action === "updatescores" && Array.isArray(data.scores)) {
      try {
        const b64 = data.jwt.split(".")[1].replace(/-/g, "+").replace(/_/g, "/");
        const jwtPayload = JSON.parse(atob(b64));
        const frameId = jwtPayload.targetid;
        const descriptor = problemRegistry[frameId];
        if (descriptor && data.scores.length > 0) {
          const current = mutable momScores;
          const updated = Object.assign({}, current);
          const raw = data.scores[0];
          updated[descriptor] = typeof raw === "number" ? String(raw) : raw;
          mutable momScores = updated;
        }
      } catch(e) { return; }
    }
  });
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
function toBase64(str) {
  return btoa(unescape(encodeURIComponent(str)));
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
mom_hash = {
  momScores;

  const scores = momScores;
  const questions = {};

  for (const [descriptor, scoreString] of Object.entries(scores)) {
    if (scoreString === "unattempted") {
      questions[descriptor] = {
        raw_scores: "unattempted",
        parts_total: null,
        parts_correct: null,
        score: null,
        result: "unattempted"
      };
    } else {
      const s = String(scoreString);
      const parts = s.includes("~")
        ? s.split("~").map(Number)
        : [parseFloat(s)];
      const total = parts.length;
      const correct = parts.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);
      const score = correct / total;
      questions[descriptor] = {
        raw_scores: s,
        parts_total: total,
        parts_correct: correct,
        score: score,
        result: score === 1 ? "fully_correct" : score === 0 ? "incorrect" : "partially_correct"
      };
    }
  }

  const payload = {
    notebook: "Prep Work: Linear Functions and Equations",
    type: "mom_questions",
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
    questions: questions
  };

  return toBase64(JSON.stringify(payload));
}
```

:::{.callout-note}
## Preparing For: Linear Functions and Equations

In our next class meeting we will investigate **linear functions** — one of the most fundamental and widely-used families of functions in mathematics. You will learn to recognize linear functions of the form $f(x) = mx + b$, interpret their slope and intercept, construct equations of lines from given information, and solve linear equations.

This prep work is designed to take approximately 20 minutes. It reviews three algebraic skills that will be called upon directly during our class discussion. Working through these problems carefully before class will help you engage more confidently with the new material.
:::

## Objectives

You'll enter our class meeting on Linear Functions and Equations able to:

- Identify the location of a point in the $xy$-plane and plot it.
- Use the slope formula to find the slope of a line connecting two points.
- Evaluate a function by substituting a numerical input and simplifying.
- Solve an equation using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division to isolate the variable.

### Just-In-Time Review

Through this section, you'll experience a refresher on several foundational skills that will be critical to our ability to analyse linear functions and equations. Take your time with the embedded problems — getting these right matters more than getting through them quickly. If you struggle on any of the skill content below, plan to 

i) ask questions at the beginning of our class meeting.
ii) visit office hours for clarification.
iii) visit the walk-in tutoring center for extra practice to master these skills.

---

#### Skill 1: Plotting Points in the Coordinate Plane

Every linear function produces a straight line when graphed in the coordinate plane. To understand what that line is doing — where it starts, how steeply it rises or falls — you need to be comfortable placing points accurately on the plane.

Recall that a point $(x, y)$ is located by moving $x$ units along the horizontal axis and $y$ units along the vertical axis. Points with negative coordinates move left or down respectively. The point $(0, 0)$ is the point at which the $x$-axis and $y$-axis intersect; it is often called the **origin**.

This skill connects directly to our class discussion because we will be reading information about linear functions from their graphs, and we will be verifying solutions to linear equations by checking whether a point lies on a given line. We'll also encounter scenarios in which we are given one or two points along a line and are asked to construct the equation describing the line. Being able to identify the location of points in the coordinate plane and to compare their positions will be critical to our success.

**Try It! 1:** Plotting points in the Plane.

<iframe 
    src="https://www.myopenmath.com/multiembedq.php?id=13775&iframe_resize_id=q1_plot_points"
    width="100%" height="575px" frameborder="0" id="mom-frame_plot_points">
</iframe>

---

#### Skill 2: Finding the Slope of a Line Through Two Points

Slope measures the **rate of change** of a linear function — how much the output changes for every one-unit increase in the input. In our class discussion, slope will be the central object of study: we will interpret it in applied contexts, compute it from data, and use it to build equations of lines.

The slope of the line connecting two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ is:

$$m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

A positive slope means the function is increasing; a negative slope means it is decreasing. A slope of zero means the function is constant. Keep an eye on the sign — it carries real meaning in context.

**Try It! 2:** Calculate the slope of the line between the two points.

<iframe 
    src="https://www.myopenmath.com/multiembedq.php?id=173601&iframe_resize_id=q2_slope_two_points"
    width="100%" height="300px" frameborder="0" id="mom-frame_slope_two_points">
</iframe>

**Try It! 3:** Calculate the slope of the line connecting the two points below.

<iframe 
    src="https://www.myopenmath.com/multiembedq.php?id=213345&iframe_resize_id=q3_slope_two_points_again"
    width="100%" height="260px" frameborder="0" id="mom-frame_slope_two_points_again">
</iframe>

---

#### Skill 3: Evaluating Functions

In our class discussion, you will encounter linear functions like $f(x) = 3x - 7$ and be asked to find outputs for specific inputs, build tables of values, and determine when a function equals a given target value. All of these tasks require fluency with function evaluation.

Recall that $f(a)$ means: **replace every occurrence of $x$ in the formula with $a$, then simplify**. The result is the output the function assigns to the input $a$.

For example, if $f(x) = 3x - 7$, then:
$$f(4) = 3(4) - 7 = 12 - 7 = 5$$

Notice that evaluating a function is different from solving an equation. When we *evaluate*, we are given the input and we find the output. When we *solve*, we are given the output and we find the input. Both tasks will appear in class.

**Try It! 4:** Evaluate the linear function for a variety of inputs.

<iframe 
    src="https://www.myopenmath.com/multiembedq.php?id=18017&iframe_resize_id=q4_evaluate_function"
    width="100%" height="285px" frameborder="0" id="mom-frame_evaluate_function">
</iframe>

You'll also be asked to solve linear equations. In doing this, remember that 

i) order of operations matters.
ii) we'll "*undo*" operations in reverse-order.
iii) subtraction "undoes" addition (and vice-versa).
iv) division "undoes" multiplication (and vice-versa).

**Try It! 5:** Evaluate the linear function and solve for the requested output.

<iframe 
    src="https://www.myopenmath.com/multiembedq.php?id=15766&showanswer=0&seed=123&iframe_resize_id=q5_evaluate_and_solve"
    width="100%" height="375px" frameborder="0" id="mom-frame_evaluate_and_solve">
</iframe>

---

### What To Expect In Class

Our class meeting opens with a real application: a gym offers memberships that include personal training sessions, and we are given pricing information for two different membership tiers. From just two data points, we will recover a complete linear model — finding the cost per session (slope) and the base membership fee ($y$-intercept) — and use it to answer pricing questions.

This application is a preview of something powerful: **two points completely determine a line**. Once you know any two input-output pairs for a linear function, you know everything about it. We will formalize this idea using the slope formula and point-slope form, then practice constructing linear equations from a variety of given information.

The second half of class turns to **linear equations** — situations where we know the output of a linear function and want to find the input. These arise constantly in applied settings: break-even analysis, distance-rate-time problems, and cost modeling all reduce to solving a linear equation.

By the end of class you will have the tools to take a real-world scenario, build a linear model from data, and use that model to answer questions — a complete problem-solving pipeline that will reappear throughout the course.

---

## Submit

If your instructor has asked you to submit this prep work, copy the hash below and submit it using the method they specified. The hash updates automatically as you complete each problem — copy it after finishing all three.

:::{.callout-warning}
## Question Hash

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div style="font-family: monospace; font-size: 0.85em; background: #f5f5f5; padding: 12px; border-radius: 6px; word-break: break-all; border: 1px solid #ddd; user-select: all; cursor: pointer;" onclick="navigator.clipboard.writeText(this.innerText)">
  ${mom_hash}
</div>
<p style="margin-top: 8px; font-size: 0.9em; color: #555;">
  Click the box to copy to clipboard.
</p>`
```

:::
